title: Writing to Files
description: Write data to files and create new files.

slides.title: Writing to Files
slides.content:
# file_put_contents() – Simple Write

The easiest way to write to a file is `file_put_contents()`. It writes a string to a file, creating the file if it doesn't exist, and overwriting existing content by default.

```php
file_put_contents('file.txt', 'Hello World!');
```

It returns the number of bytes written, or false on failure.

slides.title: Using file_put_contents
slides.codeSnippet:
<?php
$data = "This is a new line of text.";
$bytes = file_put_contents('output.txt', $data);
if ($bytes !== false) {
    echo "Successfully wrote $bytes bytes.";
} else {
    echo "Write failed.";
}
?>

slides.title: Writing with fopen() and fwrite()
Using fwrite
slides.codeSnippet:
<?php
$handle = fopen('newfile.txt', 'w');
if ($handle) {
    fwrite($handle, "First line\n");
    fwrite($handle, "Second line\n");
    fclose($handle);
    echo "File written.";
} else {
    echo "Cannot open file.";
}
?>

slides.title: File Permissions
slides.content:
## Write Permissions

Make sure the web server has write permission to the directory. On Unix systems, you may need to set permissions (e.g., `chmod 777` for testing, but for production, be more restrictive).

quizData.question: What does `file_put_contents()` return on success?
quizData.explanation: file_put_contents returns the number of bytes written, or false on error.
